text: Hello, this is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English.
zh: [0] 大家好，欢迎收听BBC英语教学的《六分钟英语》节目。

text: I'm Phil. And I'm Pippa. In this episode, we're discussing etiquette –
zh: [1] 我是Phil。我是Pippa。这一集我们要讨论的是礼仪——

text: the rules about what makes polite behaviour, and in particular, the do's and don'ts about what you should and shouldn't do when when eating.
zh: [2] 也就是关于什么是得体行为的规则，尤其是关于吃饭时该做和不该做的事情。

text: So Phil, what's the worst thing someone can do while eating, in your opinion?
zh: [3] Phil，在你看来，吃饭时人们能做的最糟糕的事情是什么？

text: For me, it's being very noisy when you do it. I can't stand it.
zh: [4] 对我来说，就是吃东西时发出很大声响，我受不了。

text: OK. I don't have too many rules about eating,
zh: [5] 好吧，我对吃饭没有太多规矩，

text: but I was always told growing up that you shouldn't have your elbows on the table, and I never really understood why, but I always remember that one.
zh: [6] 但我从小就被教导不要把胳膊放在桌子上，虽然我一直不太明白为什么，但我一直记得这一条。

text: So, we'll be hearing more about mealtime manners from some college as students and an expert on British etiquette.
zh: [7] 接下来我们会听一些大学生和一位英国礼仪专家谈谈用餐礼仪。

text: And, as usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases, all of which you can find on our website bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: [8] 像往常一样，我们也会学到一些有用的新单词和短语，都可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到。

text: But first, I have a question for you, Pippa. Ideas about polite behaviour change a lot from country to country.
zh: [9] 不过首先，Pippa，我有个问题问你。关于什么是礼貌行为的看法在不同国家差别很大。

text: So, when BBC programme The Food Chain
zh: [10] 那么，当BBC节目《The Food Chain》（食物链）

text: asked listeners from around the world about eating manners in their country, what do you think was the most common complaint?
zh: [11] 询问世界各地的听众他们国家的用餐礼仪时，你觉得最常见的抱怨是什么？

text: Was it a people eating with their hands,
zh: [12] 是a，人们用手吃饭，

text: b people eating noisily, or c people burping at the table?
zh: [13] 还是b，人们吃饭发出声响，或者c，人们在餐桌上打嗝？

text: Hmm, I don't think it's people eating with their hands because lots of people around the world do that as kind of their normal way of eating.
zh: [14] 嗯，我觉得不是用手吃饭，因为世界上很多人本来就是这样吃饭的，这是他们正常的方式。

text: But maybe it's b people eating noisily I think, like you mentioned Phil, that's quite annoying.
zh: [15] 但也许是b，吃饭发出声音，我觉得，就像你提到的Phil，那确实挺烦人的。

text: Well, we'll find out the answer later in the programme.
zh: [16] 好吧，我们稍后会揭晓答案。

text: The Food Chain also interviewed students at London's City of Westminster College about family dinner times.
zh: [17] 《The Food Chain》还采访了伦敦威斯敏斯特市学院的学生，谈论他们家庭用餐时间的情况。

text: Samuel is 19 and from Sierra Leone, and Aya is 18 and British, but her family are from Iraq.
zh: [18] Samuel今年19岁，来自塞拉利昂；Aya今年18岁，是英国人，但她的家人来自伊拉克。

text: Let's listen as they chat with BBC World Service's The Food Chain.
zh: [19] 让我们听听他们与BBC世界服务《The Food Chain》节目的对话。

text: Yeah, I would say first impressions really do matter because it also is really important and how you portray yourself, how you present yourself to the person.
zh: [20] 是的，我觉得第一印象真的很重要，因为你怎样表现自己、怎样展示给对方看，这一点也很关键。

text: What I don't want to see is any loud chewing or with your mouth open, please and thank you.
zh: [21] 我不希望看到有人大声咀嚼或者张着嘴吃东西，拜托了，谢谢。

text: I would like to see, though, eye contact, having a conversation, using your cutlery.
zh: [22] 不过我希望看到眼神交流、有交谈、正确使用餐具。

text: Sometimes, and I know this could be, like, picky for everyone else, but having a tissue or a napkin on your lap, just in case you spill anything.
zh: [23] 有时候——我知道这对别人来说可能有点吹毛求疵——但我觉得腿上放条纸巾或餐巾是有必要的，以防万一洒出来。

text: The students think etiquette is important. Samuel says it's about making a good first impression.
zh: [24] 这些学生认为礼仪很重要。Samuel说这关乎给人留下良好的第一印象。

text: Your first impression is the opinion you form about someone when you meet them for the first time.
zh: [25] 第一印象是你第一次见到某人时形成的看法。

text: For Aya, eating with your mouth open is definitely rude.
zh: [26] 对Aya来说，吃饭时张着嘴绝对是不礼貌的。

text: She also thinks people should use a napkin, although that could be seen as Picky here means being too strict about the rules.
zh: [27] 她还认为大家应该用餐巾，尽管这里可能会被认为是‘挑剔’——picky在这里指对规矩过于严格。

text: For this group of students, showing respect and appreciation for the meal is very important.
zh: [28] 对这群学生来说，对饭菜表示尊重和感激是非常重要的。

text: And this idea is common across most, if not all, cultures throughout history.
zh: [29] 而这种观念在历史上几乎所有文化中都很常见。

text: So, imagine a scene from a TV show like Game of Thrones.
zh: [30] 想象一下像《权力的游戏》这样的电视剧里的场景。

text: Two enemy forces have stopped fighting, made peace and now gather for a meal.
zh: [31] 两支敌对势力停止了战斗，达成和平，聚在一起吃饭。

text: The leaders clink glasses, meaning they lift up and touch drinks, and make a toast.
zh: [32] 双方领袖碰杯——也就是举起酒杯轻轻相碰——并祝酒。

text: They hold up a glass of alcohol and drink it to show respect. Now, keep that image in mind
zh: [33] 他们举起酒杯一饮而尽，以示尊重。现在，请记住这个画面，

text: because we're going to hear British etiquette coach Rupert Wesson explaining why people clink glasses.
zh: [34] 因为接下来我们要听英国礼仪教练Rupert Wesson解释人们为什么要碰杯。

text: Here he is talking to Ruth Alexander, presenter of BBC World Service programme, The Food Chain.
zh: [35] 他正在与BBC世界服务节目《The Food Chain》的主持人Ruth Alexander交谈。

text: Perhaps the simplest example is why do we clink glasses when we have a toast?
zh: [36] 也许最简单的例子就是，我们祝酒时为什么要碰杯？

text: If you clink glasses, you're slopping a bit of your drink into his cup and he's slopping a bit of drink into your cup.
zh: [37] 如果你碰杯，你的酒会溅一点到他的杯子里，他的酒也会溅一点到你的杯子里。

text: And therefore, by having that toast, you are showing, going, look, I trust you, you trust me, and that's why we do it.
zh: [38] 因此，通过这样的祝酒动作，你其实是在表达：看，我信任你，你也信任我，这就是我们这么做的原因。

text: If you've poisoned my drink that's now slopped into your cup, you won't drink it.
zh: [39] 如果你在我的酒里下了毒，而这毒酒又溅进了你的杯子，你就不会喝了。

text: Correct. Hats at the table. We're hearing from BBC World Service listeners that that is a no -no. Is that right?
zh: [40] 没错。那餐桌上戴帽子呢？我们听说BBC世界服务的听众认为那是不可以的，对吗？

text: It's not something I would do. In fact, I would not be wearing a hat inside anyway.
zh: [41] 那不是我会做的事。事实上，我在室内根本不会戴帽子。

text: So, Phil, when we clink glasses in a toast,
zh: [42] Phil，当我们碰杯祝酒时，

text: some of my drink might spill into your glass and some of your drink might spill into mine.
zh: [43] 我的一点酒可能会溅进你的杯子，你的一点酒也可能溅进我的杯子。

text: Historically, this mixing of drinks was a way of proving the drinks were not poisoned.
zh: [44] 从历史上看，这种酒水混合的做法是为了证明酒里没有下毒。

text: Ruth also asks about wearing hats at the table, and Rupert calls this a no -no.
zh: [45] Ruth还问到餐桌上戴帽子的问题，Rupert称这是‘no-no’（不可以做的事）。

text: A no -no is an informal phrase for something that is considered socially unacceptable.
zh: [46] no-no是一个非正式说法，指社会上不被接受的行为。

text: Etiquette varies a lot over time and culture, but the essential point is always to show appreciation by doing some things and avoiding others.
zh: [47] 礼仪随时间和文化差异很大，但核心始终是通过做某些事、避免某些事来表达尊重。

text: And speaking of things to avoid, Boyd, what was the answer to your question, Phil?
zh: [48] 说到该避免的事，Phil，你问题的答案是什么？

text: OK, well I asked you what listeners of the food chain thought was the biggest etiquette problem when people are eating.
zh: [49] 好的，我之前问你，《The Food Chain》的听众认为人们吃饭时最大的礼仪问题是什么。

text: You said that it was people eating noisily and I can reveal that's the right answer. It was people eating noisily.
zh: [50] 你说是人们吃饭发出声响，我可以告诉你，这就是正确答案。确实是吃饭发出声响。

text: OK, let's recap the vocabulary we've learnt about mealtime dos and don'ts – rules about how you should and shouldn't behave.
zh: [51] 好，让我们回顾一下今天学到的关于用餐‘该做’与‘不该做’的词汇——也就是行为规范。

text: Your first impression of someone is the opinion you form when meeting them for the first time.
zh: [52] 你对某人的第一印象，是你第一次见到他们时形成的看法。

text: The word picky is used to describe someone who is too strict about rules.
zh: [53] picky这个词用来形容一个人对规则过于严格、吹毛求疵。

text: If you clink glasses, you touch two drinking glasses together as a gesture of friendship,
zh: [54] 如果你clink glasses（碰杯），就是把两个酒杯碰在一起，作为友好的表示，

text: probably because you're making a toast – a show of respect that involves drinking from a glass of alcohol.
zh: [55] 通常是因为在祝酒——这是一种表示尊重的方式，通常要喝下杯中的酒。

text: And finally, a no -no is an informal word for something considered socially unacceptable.
zh: [56] 最后，no-no是一个非正式说法，指被认为在社会上不可接受的事情。

text: Once again, our six minutes are up, but if you'd like to find out more about dining etiquette,
zh: [57] 我们的六分钟又到了，但如果你想了解更多关于用餐礼仪的内容，

text: then head over to our website bbclearningenglish .com to download a programme worksheet and try the quiz.
zh: [58] 欢迎前往我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com，下载节目讲义并做一做测验。

text: See you again soon, but for now, it's goodbye. Bye!
zh: [59] 我们很快再见，现在先说再见啦。拜拜！

text: Six Minute English, from bbclearningenglish .com
zh: [60] 《六分钟英语》，来自bbclearningenglish.com
